author: @satibel
text: I like the flesh-like look it gives, I'd definitely use it for making some eldritch walls for miniatures.

author: @RWBHere
text:
At 9:30 , if you measure Ohms per square, then all measurements will be automatically normalised. Ask Rob to show you how to make a simple probe for the job. They only take a few minutes to construct. Everyone using the ink in the workshop would find Ohms per square useful, and it would save time for everyone. 

Thanks for the demonstration, Mandy. :-)

author: @michaelinamoment
text: What are the effects of copper plating organic tissue?

replies.author: @rawandrealserendipity
replies.text: A fabulous idea - why don't you give it a try yourself? Let us know the results.

replies.author: @michaelinamoment
replies.text: @rawandrealserendipity would be very interesting indeed. If we look at some depictions in history figures like Osiris have a green covering on them. looking at traditional green inks it's usually copper oxide of some form. so wondering whether or not they did it while person was alive or if it was a post-mortem type of thing. I'll be ordering some ink soon and will reserve some to test on perhaps a leaf or a branch of a potted plant and see if it causes permanent damage.

replies.author: @rawandrealserendipity
replies.text: Rob plated a rhododendron leaf a while ago. I saw his video. I thought you meant you wanted to copper plate a person 😂

author: @Mr.Unacceptable
text: Can you use the same ACP or ACF process to solder to this conductive ink? The same way they attach chips to aluminum antenna or LCD to glass?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome idea mate - i think very much you could and i like the idea - i think i am going to do a pcb version soon

replies.author: @Mr.Unacceptable
replies.text:
I'd like to see that even if it's failures. A little info from Scotty at Strage Parts last video of him trying to make his own RFID tags reminded me of this process. He's experimenting with the ACF trying to apply it with different methods. It's mostly his failures but still interesting.  
I want to make a wooden clock radio and a few other things with the circuit routered into the wood.  This ink might do it if the ACF process works.  The color on dark wood could look interesting. Have you tried routering a line in a piece of wood and filling it with ink, sanding it and checking to see if it still has continuance? How thin or shallow a line can you make?  Copper tape doesn't stick, glue won't take the heat, you can't sand it after and can't clean the flux off.  Lead scorches the wood and doesn't form very well in small spaces of things it doesn't stick to. My next attempt is to inlay 10 AWG copper wire. Sand one side flat and polish it and apply varnish. Then come back and scrape the varnish off the ends to solder the parts on. Cleaned with alcohol the varnish should make the flux possible to clean.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: a wooden circuit board - i like it - i'll give it a go - if we can do a circuit board on wood your clock idea would certainly work

replies.author: @zylascope
replies.text: @Mr.Unacceptable what a great idea :)

replies.author: @unlost117
replies.text: Great idea and dont make the same mistake i did. i used an old generation (1984 or so) conductive paste to make tracks but i included the load on same track. Thats was no good coz the load pulled the 0v rail up to about 3volts in my 12v cct causing issues. Keep the conductive pastes to low current paths. Use copper/aluminium/ whatever on the high current loads. Hey 50mA can be classified as high current load depending on what conductive ink you are using. Nice Work mate.
